ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Vietnamese stir-fried beef with water spinach; mapped to the closest parent class 'stir-fried food product' (FOODON_03315671).
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, stir-fried — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed stir-fried beef and vegetable dish; no exact composite dish code available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Bò xào rau muống. A0CKP (beef, cooked) used as the primary protein anchor. Vegetable component (water spinach) is not separately coded in this mapping.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this Vietnamese stir-fried dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of stir-fried beef with water spinach weighs approximately 350 g, providing around 450 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, stir-fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods) and Water spinach (Ipomoea aquatica), cooked
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for the composite dish Bò xào rau muống. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked lean beef (approximately 40% by weight), water spinach / kangkong cooked (approximately 45% by weight), and cooking oil plus condiments (approximately 15% by weight). Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and found broadly consistent; values retained with medium confidence. Sodium (342.9 mg/100g) is plausible for a restaurant preparation using fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted but no direct entry for this dish was found.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed stir-fried beef and leafy vegetable dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bò xào rau muống. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mixed stir-fried beef and leafy green vegetable dishes. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with low net carbohydrate (5.7 g/100g), which suppresses the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (32) × available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 5.7 g/100g = 20.0 g) ÷ 100 = 6.4 g GL (low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce — estimated 342.9 mg/100g, likely higher in restaurant portions
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced condiment volumes (fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce) and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, as a standard 350 g plate delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium — over half the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.7 g/100g) and high protein (10 g/100g) produce a low glycaemic load of approximately 6.4 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments; the low carbohydrate content and high protein from beef slow gastric emptying and blunt post-meal glucose response, though oyster sauce adds a small sugar load that should be minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.3 g/100g) from beef and high sodium (342.9 mg/100g) from condiments are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced condiment use to lower sodium and saturated fat intake; substituting lean beef with skinless chicken or tofu would further reduce saturated fat and support a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with chicken, tofu, or seafood to eliminate the arachidonic acid load while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefits of kangkong; chilli should be omitted by those with nightshade sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bò xào rau muống (Stir-Fried Beef with Water Spinach) is a classic Vietnamese stir-fry dish consisting of thinly sliced tender beef wok-tossed at high heat with fresh kangkong (water spinach, Ipomoea aquatica) in a savoury sauce typically made from fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and chilli. The dish is cooked rapidly over high flame to preserve the crunch of the kangkong stems and the tenderness of the beef. The flavour profile is umami-rich, slightly salty, and mildly garlicky, with a subtle sweetness from oyster sauce. Texturally, the beef is tender and juicy while the kangkong retains a pleasant bite. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 128.6 kcal, 10.0 g protein, 7.1 g fat (2.3 g saturated), 5.7 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 342.9 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 32 (low) with a glycaemic load of approximately 6.4 per standard 350 g plate, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, the high sodium content from condiments is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is a good source of iron (1.1 mg/100g) and vitamin C (4.3 mg/100g) from the kangkong. It is commonly served as a shared side dish in Vietnamese home cooking and restaurant settings across Vietnam and Southeast Asia, including Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bò xào rau muống was found in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked lean beef and cooked water spinach, cross-checked against the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). The composite estimate is broadly consistent with the input values, supporting medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese stir-fried dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100g (trace level from beef fat), consistent with USDA data for cooked lean beef. Input source listed as foodo.sg but the URL does not correspond to an approved HPB direct nutrient database entry; nutrient values re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central composite estimate. Halal status assumed based on typical Vietnamese restaurant preparation using halal-certified beef and condiments; verification recommended for specific restaurant sources.
